metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Contract section whether `HmacScheme.verify` is (a) intentionally a dead path for legacy records (POLICY pinning is metadata/routing only; verify-dispatch is never called for these kinds on pre-existing hex-HMAC records) or (b) a live path that must also bridge the legacy hex-over-canonical-JSON format — and if (a), explicitly document that the verify() method is provided for DSSE-wrapped HMAC only (future use) and add a test asserting that a legacy-format record is NOT routed through it.
findings.finding: HmacScheme.verify is specified to operate on DSSE-PAE bytes and dsse.Signature.sig (raw bytes), but every real legacy plan-review/completion-verifier record stores a hex-string HMAC over canonical JSON — not DSSE-PAE bytes and not raw bytes. The POLICY table pins both kinds to this scheme, so if the 518b registry's verify-dispatch is ever invoked for a legacy kind, it will always return mismatch/foreign_key, never certified. The plan does not clarify whether verify() is intentionally a dead path (POLICY pinning is metadata-only, dispatch is never called for these kinds on legacy records) or a live path — leaving the implementer with no authoritative spec for what HmacScheme.verify is actually for.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme.verify spec; Scope boundary paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Contract: 'NOTE: HmacScheme.verify operates on DSSE-PAE-formatted bytes only; it is not called for existing legacy records (which continue to verify via signing.py). The POLICY entries are routing metadata for the op-cert contract phase; no caller invokes registry.verify() on a legacy hex-HMAC record in this leaf.' If verify() is instead intended to be callable on legacy records, specify an additional branch: when signatures is empty but a raw hex string is provided as a fallback parameter, delegate to signing.verify_record.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Quote or inline the exact `Scheme` protocol signature (method names, parameter types, return type) from the 518b registry artifact so HmacScheme's interface can be verified against it at plan-review time, rather than relying on an in-progress dependency.
findings.finding: The plan's Scheme protocol interface (method signature `verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root) -> Verdict`) and the types it depends on (`dsse.Signature`, `registry.Verdict`) are asserted to exist in the 518b registry, but 518b is still in_progress and neither type appears anywhere in the current codebase. The plan does not cite or quote the exact interface that HmacScheme must satisfy, leaving the implementer unable to confirm the protocol is implementable without the 518b artifact.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme implements the registry Scheme protocol; Files: src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py depends on src/rebar/attest/registry.py (518b)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the encoding mismatch: specify whether `signatures[0].sig` is bytes or a hex string, and whether `HmacScheme.verify` uses `hmac.new(...).digest()` (bytes→bytes comparison) or `hmac.new(...).hexdigest()` (str→str comparison), making both sides consistent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `HmacScheme.verify` constant-time-compares `signatures[0].sig` (described as 'raw bytes') against the HMAC result, but `signing.compute_signature` returns a **hex string** (not raw bytes): `hmac.new(...).hexdigest()`. If `dsse.Signature.sig` is raw bytes (the DSSE spec stores binary signatures), `hmac.compare_digest(hex_string, bytes)` raises TypeError. The plan does not address this encoding mismatch.
findings.location: Contract section: 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over pae_bytes... constant-time-compares (hmac.compare_digest) to signatures[0].sig (the raw bytes of the first dsse.Signature)'
findings.suggested_fix: If `dsse.Signature.sig` is raw bytes, use `hmac.new(key, pae_bytes, hashlib.sha256).digest()` and compare with `hmac.compare_digest(computed_bytes, signatures[0].sig)`. Update the round-trip helper in tests to set `signatures[0].sig = hmac.new(key, pae_bytes, hashlib.sha256).digest()` (not hexdigest).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify (in a doc comment or plan note) that POLICY entries for `plan-review`/`completion-verifier` are forward-looking stubs for future DSSE-enveloped records, NOT usable for verifying existing legacy records, and that consumers must check record shape before choosing the verification path.
findings.finding: The POLICY entries pin legacy kinds (`plan-review`, `completion-verifier`) to `HmacScheme`, but `HmacScheme.verify` operates over DSSE-PAE bytes — a different payload format than what existing legacy records were signed over (canonical `{v,algorithm,ticket_id,manifest}`). A consumer that calls the registry to verify a legacy record under these POLICY entries will get a false `mismatch` verdict, even though the record is valid and verifies correctly through `signing.py`.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme.verify; Scope boundary section; AC line 1 (POLICY entries)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prominent comment to the POLICY entries (or a `verify_legacy` method) that documents: these POLICY entries bind the *kind* to the HMAC scheme for future DSSE-wrapped records only; legacy hex-HMAC records over the canonical payload must still be dispatched to `signing.verify_signature`. Alternatively, rename or annotate the policy entries as `_FUTURE_POLICY` to prevent accidental misuse.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that ticket 518b (`src/rebar/attest/registry.py`) is merged and its API (`Scheme` protocol, `get_scheme()`, `resolve()`, `POLICY`) matches the names used in this plan's Contract and AC before beginning implementation.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `src/rebar/attest/registry.py` (task 518b) exists and exposes `Scheme` protocol, `get_scheme()`, `resolve()`, and `POLICY` dict — but the file does not exist in the repo (the attest/ package itself is absent), and ticket 518b's own plan-review manifest records 'dep absent src/rebar/attest/registry.py', confirming it was not committed at review time. The plan's entire implementation depends on these APIs without acknowledging the open dependency.
findings.location: Context section ('task 518b'), Contract section ('implements the registry Scheme protocol'), AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite gate: 'Blocked on 518b merge; confirm `registry.get_scheme`, `registry.resolve`, and `POLICY` are the exact exported names before coding `register_legacy_schemes()`.' If 518b defines a different API (e.g., `verify(kind, ...)` dispatch only), update the Contract and AC to match.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `depends_on` link to task 518b so the registry symbols (`Scheme`, `Verdict`, `Policy`, `get_scheme`, `resolve`) are guaranteed to exist before this ticket's implementation begins.
findings.finding: The ticket's `deps` field is empty (`[]`), meaning `registry.Scheme`, `registry.Verdict`, `registry.Policy`, `registry.get_scheme`, and `registry.resolve` — all defined by sibling task 518b (`src/rebar/attest/registry.py`) — are not reachable via the rubric's recursive deps-exist exemption. These symbols are referenced throughout the Contract section and AC1 but are unresolvable at the time this ticket is implemented unless 518b completes first.
findings.location: Contract section; AC1; ticket `deps: []`
findings.suggested_fix: Register ticket 518b-0558-fd18-4713 in this ticket's `deps` field as `depends_on`. The plan text already acknowledges 518b; formalizing the link closes the ordering gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `depends_on` link to the DSSE envelope + PAE sibling task (child 1 of epic 2fd4) so that `dsse.Signature` is guaranteed to exist before this ticket's implementation; or, if the DSSE type import is optional/lazy, document how the test helper constructs `signatures` without it.
findings.finding: `dsse.Signature` — referenced in the Contract section as the type of `signatures[0]` in `HmacScheme.verify` — is provided by a separate sibling ticket (child 1 of epic 2fd4: 'DSSE envelope + PAE module'), which is also absent from this ticket's `deps` and not resolvable in the installed environment.
findings.location: Contract section: 'signatures[0].sig (the raw bytes of the first dsse.Signature)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Spell out the complete `Verdict` constructor call in the Contract section (all positional/keyword args), or add a reference to the exact Verdict dataclass definition in registry.py so the executor need not look up a sibling ticket.
findings.finding: The `Verdict` constructor is called as `Verdict(True,"certified",...)` and `Verdict(False,"mismatch",...)` with trailing `...`, leaving any additional required constructor fields unspecified. If `Verdict` (defined in task 518b's registry) has positional or keyword fields beyond `(ok, reason)`, the executor must guess their values or go look them up in a sibling ticket.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme.verify return values
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `Verdict(True,"certified",...)` with the full call, e.g. `Verdict(ok=True, reason="certified", scheme="HMAC-SHA256")` (adjust field names to match the actual dataclass); do the same for the mismatch and foreign_key cases.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the `trust_root` type annotation and enumerate which values count as 'empty/missing' (e.g. `Optional[bytes]`; `None` or `b''` both trigger `foreign_key`) in the Contract section.
findings.finding: The `trust_root` parameter type for `HmacScheme.verify` is unspecified. The plan describes 'empty/missing key' behavior, implying it can be `None`, `b''`, or `''`, but does not state the type annotation or which empty-key variants trigger `foreign_key`.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme.verify signature
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the contract: '`trust_root: Optional[bytes]` — `None` or `b""` both trigger the `foreign_key` fail-closed path; a non-empty bytes value is the key material.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the 'HMAC registered as legacy scheme, asymmetric cutover deferred to op-cert epic' architectural decision, including the scope boundary and the invariant that signing.py remains unchanged.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — registering a symmetric HMAC scheme as a first-class 'legacy' entry in the attest registry alongside (future) asymmetric schemes — but includes no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture the rationale, the scope boundary, or the future cutover contract.
findings.location: Context section / Files list (no ADR/CLAUDE.md file listed)
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/NNN-hmac-legacy-registry.md` (or add a section to CLAUDE.md) documenting: (1) why HMAC is registered rather than left entirely outside the registry, (2) the invariant that old records continue to verify through signing.py unchanged, (3) the intended cutover trigger and which epic owns it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the registry (task 518b) guarantees atomic/thread-safe registration (e.g., via a lock or idempotent dict assignment), or document that `register_legacy_schemes()` assumes single-threaded import context and must not be called concurrently.
findings.finding: The plan requires `register_legacy_schemes()` to be idempotent (calling twice leaves exactly one scheme and two POLICY entries), but does not specify whether the registry's internal mutation is atomic or thread-safe. If the registry uses a check-then-act pattern (check if scheme exists, then insert) without a lock, two threads calling `register_legacy_schemes()` concurrently could both pass the check and produce duplicate entries or a corrupted registry state.
findings.location: Contract section — register_legacy_schemes() idempotency; Files — src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether HmacScheme.verify needs a full DSSE-PAE interface in this expand leaf, or whether a stub/not-implemented body (deferring the PAE-bytes contract to the op-cert epic that will actually call it) avoids a premature-abstraction finding under Rule-of-Three.
findings.finding: HmacScheme.verify is specified with a DSSE-PAE input interface (pae_bytes, dsse.Signature objects) that has zero existing call-sites in the codebase and cannot verify any existing legacy record, making it dead code in this leaf and a speculative interface for the out-of-scope op-cert epic.
findings.location: Contract section: 'HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)' + Scope boundary: 'Existing ... attestations ... keep verifying UNCHANGED through the untouched signing.py path'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether HmacScheme needs a full verify() implementation in this expand phase (given legacy records never route through it), or whether the leaf's value is purely the registry registration + POLICY pinning, with verify() deferred to when DSSE envelopes are actually introduced.
findings.finding: Forcing HMAC into the asymmetric-oriented Scheme protocol interface (with namespace and trust_root parameters derived from the DSSE/asymmetric design) is a golden-hammer pattern: the HMAC verification path that actually runs in production uses signing.verify_record and will continue to do so — the Scheme.verify shape serves a future envelope format, not the legacy records this leaf is nominally about.
findings.location: Contract section: 'HmacScheme implements the registry Scheme protocol' + Files: src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Identify and confirm the concrete source of the `dsse.Signature` type (and `pae_bytes` format) that `HmacScheme.verify` depends on — either as a 518b-defined local type, an installed third-party package, or a stdlib substitute — and update the Contract to reference it unambiguously.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, ...)` operates on `dsse.Signature` objects (plan: 'the raw bytes of the first `dsse.Signature`'), but `dsse` is not importable in the installed environment, there are zero `dsse` or DSSE-PAE references anywhere in the codebase (only one doc-string mention in test_snapshot_docs.py), and no `dsse` package is installed. This is an unverified dependency on an absent library.
findings.location: Contract section: 'HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)... signatures[0].sig (the raw bytes of the first dsse.Signature)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether HmacScheme.verify signs over DSSE-PAE bytes or the legacy canonical-JSON payload (`signing._canonical_payload`), and resolve the contradiction: if pae_bytes is truly the input, document explicitly that HmacScheme cannot verify stored legacy records (making the POLICY pin forward-only); if the intent is legacy compatibility, change the input to canonical-JSON bytes and explain how the Scheme protocol accommodates a non-PAE input.
findings.finding: HmacScheme.verify is specified to HMAC over 'pae_bytes' (DSSE pre-authentication encoding bytes), but legacy signature records in the store were computed via signing._canonical_payload (canonical JSON of {v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest}), not PAE-encoded bytes. These two payload formats produce different digests, so HmacScheme.verify as specified cannot verify any existing legacy record — contradicting the plan's stated goal of registering the HMAC primitive that underlies those legacy attestations.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme.verify signature; also Files: src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the origin and import path of `dsse.Signature` used in `HmacScheme.verify`: if it is an external package, add it to pyproject.toml and document the dependency; if it is an internal type defined in task-518b's `registry.py`, name the module path explicitly in this plan.
findings.finding: The plan specifies `HmacScheme.verify` accepts `signatures: list[dsse.Signature]` and computes HMAC over `pae_bytes` (DSSE Pre-Authentication Encoding bytes), but neither a `dsse` package nor any DSSE-PAE encoding utility is present in pyproject.toml dependencies, used anywhere in the existing codebase, or resolvable in the installed environment. This is a new library/format integration asserted without verification.
findings.location: Contract section — `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)` and `signatures[0].sig`
findings.suggested_fix: In the Contract section, replace the bare `dsse.Signature` reference with the full qualified path (e.g., `rebar.attest.registry.Signature` if it's internal, or `dsse.Signature` with a `pyproject.toml` entry and `[project.optional-dependencies]` group if external). If `Signature` is simply a `dataclass(sig: bytes, keyid: str)` defined in registry.py, spell that out so the implementation is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Contract how HmacScheme.verify bridges the DSSE-PAE `pae_bytes` interface to the legacy `_canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest)` payload: either (a) document that verify re-derives the canonical payload from structured fields (not raw pae_bytes) for legacy records, or (b) clarify that HmacScheme.verify is only used for future DSSE-wrapped records and add an AC that verifies it certifies an existing legacy-shaped record correctly.
findings.finding: HmacScheme.verify is specified to compute HMAC-SHA256 over `pae_bytes` (described as 'DSSE-PAE bytes'), but all existing legacy records store HMAC over `_canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest)` — a compact JSON blob with no DSSE-PAE framing. The plan never specifies the protocol bridging these two payload formats, leaving it undefined how HmacScheme.verify can ever certify a real legacy record. The AC regression test only exercises signing.verify_signature (the old path), never HmacScheme.verify against a legacy record, so this structural gap is undetected by the specified tests.
findings.location: Contract section — HmacScheme.verify signature and pae_bytes parameter; AC item 2 (fail-closed matrix); AC item 4 (regression guard)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Contract: 'For the legacy scheme, verify re-derives the signed payload via signing._canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest) extracted from the DSSE envelope's payload field (not by HMACing the raw PAE bytes), and compares to signatures[0].sig. The pae_bytes parameter is accepted for protocol conformance but is not the HMAC input.' Add an AC item: 'HmacScheme.verify certifies a real legacy-shaped record (signature = hmac_sha256(key, canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest))) when called with the matching key.'

findings.finding: The HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root) dispatch contract uses a DSSE-style interface (pre-serialised bytes + a signatures list with .sig objects) that does not match the shape the legacy HMAC path actually needs: signing.compute_signature requires ticket_id and manifest as separate arguments to reconstruct _canonical_payload(ticket_id, manifest), and legacy records carry 'signature' as a flat hex string — not as signatures[0].sig. The plan leaves unspecified (a) how the registry caller produces pae_bytes for the HMAC scheme (who calls _canonical_payload, and with what ticket_id/manifest?), and (b) how signatures[0].sig is derived from a legacy record dict. An implementer following the plan verbatim will build an adapter whose input shapes don't match what signing.py actually consumes.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section — HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)
findings.suggested_fix: In the Dispatch contract, replace the abstract pae_bytes/signatures description with a concrete one: HmacScheme.verify receives the legacy record dict + ticket_id (matching signing.verify_record's actual call signature), derives the expected hex by calling signing.compute_signature(ticket_id, record['manifest'], trust_root), and compares it to record.get('signature','') via hmac.compare_digest. This keeps the adapter faithful to the existing path with no reimplementation.

findings.finding: The plan's `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root)` dispatch contract asserts it 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over `pae_bytes`', but the legacy HMAC in `signing.py` computes over `_canonical_payload()` (canonical JSON bytes of `{v, algorithm, ticket_id, manifest}`), not DSSE Pre-Authentication Encoding (PAE) bytes. No PAE implementation exists anywhere in the codebase.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section: 'HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, namespace, trust_root) computes HMAC-SHA256 over pae_bytes'

findings.finding: The dispatch contract asserts `HmacScheme.verify` constant-time-compares to `signatures[0].sig`, but existing legacy HMAC records store the signature as `record["signature"]` (a plain hex string), not a `signatures` array. No `signatures` array structure exists in any legacy record or in `signing.py`.
findings.location: Dispatch contract section: 'constant-time-compares (hmac.compare_digest) to signatures[0].sig'

findings.finding: `HmacScheme.verify(pae_bytes, signatures, ...)` is specified to compute HMAC-SHA256 over DSSE PAE bytes (`pae_bytes`) and compare to `signatures[0].sig`, but ALL existing stored HMAC signatures were computed over `_canonical_payload` (sorted-key compact JSON via `canonical_bytes`), not PAE bytes. Routing a legacy record through the new `HmacScheme` via the registry dispatch would always produce `mismatch`, contradicting AC2 (test matrix) and AC3/AC4 (regression: existing attestations must return `certified` via the new path).
findings.location: Dispatch contract section / AC2 / AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the dispatch contract, replace 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over pae_bytes' with 'computes HMAC-SHA256 over the legacy canonical JSON payload (via signing._canonical_payload / signing.compute_signature), NOT over PAE bytes — HmacScheme is an intentional anomaly that wraps the existing signing.py logic verbatim so existing records verify correctly.' Adjust the `verify` signature to accept the manifest and ticket_id needed to reconstruct the canonical payload, or simply delegate to `signing.verify_record`.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
